title: Portfolio
text:
Introduction
This is a Quarto book made by Matt.
I currently work in a GIS-focused role within local government at Brent Council, where much of my work centres around address data, UPRNs, and improving data quality across systems. This has given me practical experience in managing spatial data, working with tools such as QGIS, PostGIS, GeoServer, and FME, and understanding how geospatial data feeds into real-world decision making and service delivery.
My background is more applied than theoretical, with a strong focus on solving operational problems rather than developing analytical methods from first principles. Because of this, I am particularly interested in how remote sensing and earth observation data can be integrated into existing workflows, rather than treated as standalone academic outputs.
Through this module, I am hoping to develop a better understanding of how earth observation data can be used in a practical and scalable way within local government. In particular, I am interested in how these data sources could support areas such as environmental monitoring, infrastructure planning, and risk management, and how they could be incorporated into existing data pipelines.
I am also keen to improve how I communicate technical work, particularly through tools like Quarto and Xaringan, as presenting complex information clearly is a key part of my role. Overall, I am looking to bridge the gap between academic approaches to remote sensing and the constraints and realities of working within a public sector environment such as Brent.
To learn more about Quarto books visit https://quarto.org/docs/books.

title: 1  Getting Started with remote sensing
text: 1.1 Weekly Learning Diary
crumbs: 1  Week 1 – An Introduction to Remote Sensing

title: 1  Getting Started with remote sensing
text:
Summary



This week provided an introduction to remote sensing as a concept, focusing on how the Earth can be observed without direct contact. The lecture explored how sensors can be mounted on different platforms, including satellites, aircraft, and drones, and introduced the distinction between passive and active sensing. Passive sensors rely on naturally occurring radiation such as sunlight, while active sensors emit their own signal; however, both approaches have limitations, including dependency on environmental conditions for passive systems and increased complexity and cost for active systems.
A key focus was on electromagnetic radiation and how it interacts with the Earth’s surface, producing distinct spectral signatures that allow different materials to be identified. This was supported by discussion of atmospheric processes such as scattering, as well as practical challenges including cloud cover and varying spatial, temporal, and spectral resolutions. Together, these concepts highlighted both the potential of remote sensing for large-scale observation and the constraints that affect data quality and interpretation.
The practical aimed to apply these principles through working with Landsat and Sentinel imagery. This included sourcing and comparing datasets, undertaking basic raster processing and statistics, evaluating the advantages and disadvantages of different software, and analysing spectral signatures. Initially, getting to grips with Copernicus was challenging due to the number of options and the process of identifying and downloading suitable data, which was also slow. I then found SNAP difficult to use, particularly when loading and processing the data, which limited my ability to fully complete the workflow. However, using R to explore spectral signatures helped reinforce how remotely sensed data can be analysed quantitatively rather than purely visually.
However, several limitations were evident. Remote sensing is highly dependent on data availability and quality, with cloud cover, atmospheric effects, and resolution all impacting reliability. In addition, the complexity of accessing and processing data, as well as the usability of tools such as SNAP, can present barriers to effective use. These challenges highlight the importance of both data considerations and technical capability when applying remote sensing in real-world contexts.








Applications



Remote sensing plays a key role in environmental and urban monitoring by allowing large areas to be observed consistently over time. One of its most common applications is land use and land cover classification, where spectral signatures are used to distinguish between materials such as vegetation, water, and built-up areas. For example, satellite imagery from platforms such as Landsat has been widely used to map urban expansion and monitor land use change over time, as demonstrated in long-term satellite analyses (e.g. Wulder et al. (2018)). This ability to consistently observe change supports decision-making in areas such as urban planning and environmental management.
These techniques are also applied in environmental monitoring, where differences in spectral response can indicate changes in vegetation health or water quality. Vegetation indices derived from satellite imagery, such as NDVI, have been widely used to assess ecosystem condition and detect environmental change (e.g. Pettorelli et al. (2005)). In practice, similar principles are also applied using other remote sensing data sources such as LiDAR. For example, in a planning enforcement context, I have used LiDAR data to measure changes in surface height in order to provide evidence of unauthorised development. This highlights how remotely sensed data can be applied in regulatory and decision-making processes. However, the effectiveness of these applications is influenced by factors such as atmospheric interference, cloud cover, and the spatial and temporal resolution of the data. This highlights the importance of selecting appropriate data sources and understanding their limitations when applying remote sensing in real-world contexts.








Reflection



This week helped build a clearer understanding of how remote sensing moves from theory into practice, particularly how different data sources and tools influence what can actually be achieved. While the concepts themselves were relatively straightforward, I found the practical more challenging, especially when working with SNAP. Difficulties loading and processing the data made it harder to follow the full workflow, which highlighted how important usability and reliability are when working with real-world datasets. In contrast, I found working in R more intuitive, reinforcing my preference for coding-based approaches where processes are more transparent and reproducible.
What stood out most was how remote sensing enables large-scale and repeatable analysis that would not be feasible through traditional data collection methods. In a local government context, this has clear value. Access to satellite data through sources such as the Earth Observation Data Hub (EODH), alongside other remotely sensed datasets available under the Public Sector Geospatial Agreement (PSGA), creates opportunities to monitor environmental change, urban development, and land use more consistently across the borough. This could support areas such as planning, enforcement, and environmental monitoring by providing an additional layer of evidence to complement existing GIS datasets.
However, this week also made it clear that access to data alone is not enough. The challenges experienced during the practical demonstrated that the choice of tools and the ability to process and interpret the data are equally important. Moving forward, I would be more inclined to explore platforms such as Google Earth Engine or integrate remote sensing into existing GIS and database workflows, where analysis can be scaled and automated more effectively. Overall, this week highlighted both the potential and the practical limitations of applying remote sensing in real-world settings.
crumbs: 1  Week 1 – An Introduction to Remote Sensing

title: 3  Remote Sensing Data
text:
3.1 Weekly Learning Diary
:::
crumbs: 3  Week 3 – Remote Sensing Data

title: 3  Remote Sensing Data
text:
Summary



This week focused on the importance of preparing remote sensing data for analysis, emphasising that raw satellite imagery cannot be used reliably without preprocessing. Key concepts included radiometric correction, which adjusts pixel values to account for sensor inconsistencies and atmospheric effects, atmospheric correction to reduce the impact of scattering and absorption, and geometric correction to ensure imagery is accurately aligned with real-world coordinates. These processes are essential for making data comparable across time and between different sensors.
The practical demonstrated how these corrections are applied in practice, particularly through working with satellite imagery where raw pixel values were transformed into more meaningful surface reflectance values. This made it clear that without correction, the same location could appear different across images due to atmospheric conditions or sensor variation rather than actual change on the ground. Applying these steps showed how preprocessing directly affects the reliability of any subsequent analysis, such as classification or change detection.
However, the process also highlighted practical challenges. Correction workflows involve multiple steps that must be applied in the correct order, and small mistakes can significantly affect the final output. In addition, processing large raster datasets can be slow and computationally demanding, particularly when working outside of cloud-based environments. This makes it more difficult to scale these methods or apply them consistently without more automated approaches. While in my current role the geographic focus is relatively small, at the scale of a London borough, processing these datasets can still be resource intensive. This highlights the importance of improving both efficiency and workflow design when working with remote sensing data in real-world contexts.








Applications



Correction processes are a critical step in making remote sensing data usable within operational workflows, particularly where consistency and accuracy are required across datasets. In practice, these corrections are applied as part of a preprocessing pipeline, preparing imagery for tasks such as classification, change detection, or spatial analysis. For example, converting raw imagery to surface reflectance ensures that pixel values represent actual ground conditions rather than variations caused by atmospheric effects or sensor differences, allowing datasets to be meaningfully compared. This is a standard requirement in remote sensing workflows, where surface reflectance products are widely used to improve analytical reliability (e.g. Vermote et al. (2016)).
A key application of these processes is the integration of satellite imagery with existing GIS systems. For remotely sensed data to be used alongside other spatial datasets, such as vector boundaries, infrastructure layers, or land use data, it must be both spatially aligned and radiometrically consistent. Geometric correction ensures that imagery accurately overlays with other datasets, while radiometric and atmospheric corrections ensure that any analysis carried out is based on comparable and reliable inputs. Without these steps, misalignment or inconsistent pixel values could lead to errors in spatial analysis or misinterpretation of results. The importance of geometric accuracy in ensuring interoperability between datasets has been widely recognised in geospatial data integration (e.g. Toutin (2004)).
These corrections also enable the development of repeatable and scalable workflows. When remote sensing is used as part of a wider analytical pipeline, preprocessing must be applied consistently to ensure that outputs are comparable across time and between datasets. This is particularly important when automating processes such as monitoring change or updating spatial models, where inconsistencies in input data can propagate through the workflow and affect final outputs. As a result, correction processes form a foundational component of integrating remote sensing into broader data systems, enabling it to function as a reliable input rather than a standalone dataset.








Reflection



This week shifted my perspective on remote sensing from focusing on the data itself to understanding the importance of how that data is prepared before analysis. It became clear that raw imagery cannot be taken at face value, and that preprocessing steps such as radiometric, atmospheric, and geometric correction are essential for ensuring that the data is both accurate and comparable. This highlighted that much of the reliability of any analysis is determined before the analysis itself even begins.
Through the practical, I found that applying these corrections required a more structured and methodical approach than previous tasks. The need to follow a defined sequence of steps reinforced how sensitive these workflows are to error, and how easily results could be affected by incorrect processing. This made the process feel less intuitive than earlier exercises, but also emphasised the importance of understanding what each step is doing rather than simply following instructions.
What I found particularly relevant was how these preprocessing steps fit into wider analytical workflows. Rather than viewing remote sensing as a standalone process, this week highlighted its role as part of a broader data pipeline, where consistency and standardisation are critical. From my perspective, this aligns closely with how spatial data is managed more generally, where ensuring data quality and compatibility is essential before any meaningful analysis can take place.
As my experience with remote sensing develops, this has also highlighted an opportunity within my current role. Remote sensing is not yet widely embedded within existing workflows, which creates a relatively open starting point for how it could be implemented. This presents an opportunity to establish processes that are structured, efficient, and scalable from the outset, rather than adapting or retrofitting existing approaches.
Looking ahead, this has reinforced the importance of developing more efficient and repeatable methods for handling remote sensing data. While the tools used in the practical demonstrated the concepts effectively, they also highlighted limitations in terms of scalability and usability. I would be interested in exploring more automated or code-based approaches, where preprocessing steps can be integrated into larger workflows and applied consistently across datasets. Overall, this week demonstrated that the value of remote sensing is not just in the data itself, but in how effectively it is processed and integrated into analytical systems.
crumbs: 3  Week 3 – Remote Sensing Data

title: 4  Policy Applications / Temperature and Policy
text:
4.1 Weekly Learning Diary
:::
crumbs: 4  Week 4 & 5 – Policy Applications / Temperature and Policy

title: 4  Policy Applications / Temperature and Policy
text:
Summary



This week focused on the application of remote sensing within policy and decision-making contexts, highlighting the challenges of translating Earth Observation data into actionable insights. While remote sensing provides large-scale, consistent, and repeatable data, its value depends on how effectively it can be interpreted and communicated to support real-world decisions. Key themes included the role of remote sensing in monitoring environmental change, the importance of scale when applying data to policy contexts, and the need to balance scientific accuracy with usability.
The lectures demonstrated how remotely sensed data can be used to inform areas such as climate monitoring, urban heat analysis, and environmental management. However, they also emphasised that there is often a disconnect between the complexity of the data and the needs of decision-makers, who require clear, accessible outputs rather than raw or highly technical datasets. This creates a challenge in ensuring that data is both robust and usable within policy environments.
A key part of the session also involved discussion around how remote sensing outputs can be positioned and communicated to organisations or decision-makers. This included exploring how to present research as a service or product, and the importance of aligning outputs with the needs of stakeholders. Contributions from those of us already working in professional roles, particularly in or with climate-related fields, provided insight into how these approaches are applied in practice, emphasising the importance of clear communication, relevance, and usability when engaging with organisations along with presenting the best value for money.
The practical explored these challenges through applying remote sensing data within a policy-focused context, reinforcing the importance of translating technical outputs into meaningful indicators that can support decision-making. This highlighted that the effectiveness of remote sensing is not just determined by data quality, but by how well it is communicated and integrated into policy processes.
However, several limitations were evident. Differences in spatial and temporal scale can make it difficult to align remote sensing outputs with policy boundaries or reporting requirements. In addition, uncertainty within the data and methods can make it challenging to present results with confidence in decision-making contexts. These challenges highlight the importance of not only producing accurate data, but also ensuring that it is appropriately interpreted and communicated when used to inform policy.








Applications



The application of remote sensing within policy contexts relies on its ability to provide consistent, large-scale observations that can support evidence-based decision-making. In practice, this often involves translating complex datasets into simplified indicators that can be used by non-specialist stakeholders. For example, satellite-derived measures such as land surface temperature or vegetation cover are frequently used to identify areas of environmental risk, helping to prioritise interventions or inform policy development.
A key aspect of this process is the translation of data into formats that align with policy needs. Rather than working directly with raw imagery or technical outputs, remote sensing data is often aggregated, classified, or converted into metrics that can be more easily interpreted. This enables it to be integrated into decision-making frameworks, where it can be used alongside other datasets to support planning, environmental monitoring, or resource allocation. The importance of this translation has been widely recognised, with research highlighting the need to bridge the gap between Earth Observation data and policy application (e.g. European Commission / Copernicus).
Remote sensing is also increasingly used to support climate-related policy, where its ability to provide repeatable and comparable data over time is particularly valuable. For example, satellite observations are used to monitor urban heat, track environmental change, and assess the effectiveness of interventions. However, the effectiveness of these applications depends not only on the quality of the data, but on how well it is communicated and aligned with the needs of decision-makers. This reflects the broader challenge of ensuring that technical outputs are translated into actionable insights, enabling remote sensing to function as a practical tool within policy and governance contexts rather than remaining a purely analytical resource.








Reflection



This week highlighted a shift from understanding remote sensing as a technical process to recognising its role within decision-making and policy contexts. What became clear is that producing technically robust outputs is only part of the challenge, and that their value depends on how they are positioned and used within real-world settings.
One aspect I found particularly relevant was the discussion around how to engage organisations and present remote sensing outputs in a way that demonstrates their practical value. Rather than focusing solely on translating complex data into simpler formats, there is also a need to actively communicate what is possible with the data. In some cases, organisations may not be aware of the capabilities available internally, which can lead to reliance on external consultants for work that could potentially be delivered in-house. This highlights the importance of not only producing analysis, but also advocating for its use and demonstrating its relevance.
From my own experience, this reflects a broader challenge within local government, where new approaches are not always embedded into existing workflows. This creates an opportunity to introduce remote sensing in a way that is both practical and aligned with operational needs, rather than being seen as an additional or external process. It also reinforces the importance of building confidence in these methods by clearly demonstrating their value and reliability.
Looking ahead, this has influenced how I think about applying remote sensing within my own role. It is not just about developing technical capability, but about ensuring that this capability is visible, understood, and used effectively. This includes identifying where remote sensing can add value, integrating it into existing processes, and communicating its benefits in a way that supports decision-making. Overall, this week demonstrated that the successful application of remote sensing depends not only on the data and methods, but on how effectively its value is recognised and utilised within an organisation.
crumbs: 4  Week 4 & 5 – Policy Applications / Temperature and Policy

title: 5  Google Earth Engine
text:
5.1 Weekly Learning Diary
:::
crumbs: 5  Week 6 – An Introduction to Google Earth Engine

title: 5  Google Earth Engine
text:
Summary



This week introduced Google Earth Engine (GEE) as a cloud-based platform for processing and analysing remote sensing data, representing a shift away from traditional desktop-based workflows. Rather than downloading and manually processing large datasets, GEE enables analysis to be performed directly on remote servers, allowing access to extensive satellite archives such as Landsat and Sentinel without the need for local storage or significant computational resources.
The lectures highlighted how this approach enables more efficient and scalable analysis, particularly when working with large spatial extents or time-series data. By using a scripting-based environment, workflows can be automated and applied consistently across datasets, reducing the need for repetitive manual processing. This represents a significant advantage over traditional methods, where data handling and processing constraints can limit the scope of analysis.
The practical demonstrated these capabilities through working with satellite imagery in GEE, including filtering datasets, applying basic processing steps, and visualising outputs. This reinforced how quickly large volumes of data can be accessed and analysed compared to desktop-based approaches.
However, several limitations were also evident. The use of a scripting environment introduces a learning curve, particularly for those less familiar with coding, and requires a different way of thinking compared to graphical interfaces. In addition, working within a cloud-based system can reduce visibility over underlying processes, making it important to understand how data is being handled and processed. While GEE is currently free to use, there are increasing indications that usage limits and potential costs may be introduced, which could affect its accessibility in the future. This highlights the importance of considering long-term sustainability and dependency when adopting cloud-based platforms for remote sensing analysis.








Applications



The application of platforms such as Google Earth Engine lies in their ability to enable large-scale and efficient analysis of remote sensing data, particularly where traditional desktop-based approaches would be too slow or resource-intensive. By removing the need to download and locally process large datasets, GEE allows users to work directly with extensive satellite archives, making it possible to analyse changes over large areas and long time periods in a relatively short amount of time.
One key application of this is in environmental monitoring, where GEE has been used to analyse global-scale datasets such as deforestation and land cover change. For example, large-scale forest monitoring projects use satellite imagery processed in cloud environments to detect changes over time, enabling near real-time identification of deforestation events. This would be difficult to achieve using traditional workflows due to the volume of data involved.
GEE is also widely used in urban and climate-related analysis, where time-series data can be used to assess trends such as urban expansion or changes in land surface temperature. The ability to rapidly filter, process, and visualise data allows for more dynamic analysis compared to static desktop-based approaches, particularly when working with multiple datasets over time.
In addition to analysis, GEE also enables the development of interactive applications that allow users to engage directly with processed data. These applications can provide a front-end interface where non-technical users can explore outputs, adjust parameters, and visualise results without needing to interact with the underlying code. This extends the use of remote sensing beyond analysts, making it more accessible to decision-makers and stakeholders.
A key advantage of these applications is the ability to develop repeatable and scalable workflows. Once a process has been defined, it can be applied across different locations or time periods with minimal additional effort, supporting more efficient and consistent analysis. This highlights how platforms such as GEE enable remote sensing to move beyond small-scale, manual analysis and become a more integrated and scalable tool within wider analytical workflows.








Reflection



This week highlighted a clear shift towards more efficient and scalable approaches to remote sensing analysis through the use of Google Earth Engine. Compared to previous tools such as SNAP, I found GEE significantly more accessible and intuitive, particularly due to its scripting-based environment. Having a prior understanding of JavaScript made it easier to engage with the platform, and the ability to write and modify code directly provided a greater sense of control over the analysis process.
What stood out most was the speed and efficiency with which large datasets could be accessed and processed. Tasks that would typically require downloading and managing large volumes of data could instead be completed within a single script, highlighting the advantages of cloud-based workflows. However, this also raised some limitations. While scripting provides flexibility, it requires a clear understanding of the syntax and structure, and I found that AI-based tools such as large language models were not always reliable when generating GEE-specific code. This reinforces the importance of understanding the underlying logic rather than relying entirely on automated assistance.
From a practical perspective, I can see clear opportunities to apply this approach within my own work, particularly for analysing change over time or working with larger datasets more efficiently. The ability to develop repeatable workflows and quickly generate outputs would provide a significant improvement over more manual methods. However, it is also important to recognise the constraints around data security. As a cloud-based platform, GEE is not suitable for handling sensitive or restricted data, meaning that its use would need to be carefully considered within organisational policies and data governance frameworks.
Looking ahead, this week has reinforced the value of moving towards more automated and scalable approaches to remote sensing. While traditional tools still have their place, platforms such as GEE offer a more efficient way to work with large and complex datasets. Overall, this has influenced how I approach remote sensing, shifting the focus towards workflows that prioritise efficiency, reproducibility, and integration into wider analytical processes.
crumbs: 5  Week 6 – An Introduction to Google Earth Engine

title: 6  Classification
text:
6.1 Weekly Learning Diary
:::
crumbs: 6  Week 7 – Classification

title: 6  Classification
text:
Summary



This week introduced classification techniques in remote sensing, focusing on how satellite imagery can be converted into meaningful categories such as land cover types. Rather than working directly with raw imagery, classification enables pixels to be grouped based on their spectral characteristics, allowing for more interpretable outputs that can be used in analysis and decision-making.
Key concepts included the distinction between supervised and unsupervised classification. Supervised classification involves training the model using known sample data, allowing it to assign classes based on predefined categories, while unsupervised classification groups pixels based on natural patterns within the data without prior labelling. These approaches highlight different ways of interpreting imagery, each with its own advantages and limitations depending on the context and available data.
The practical explored these methods through applying classification techniques to satellite imagery, including defining training areas and generating classified outputs. This demonstrated how the choice of training data and classification method directly influences the final result, reinforcing the importance of careful input selection.
However, several limitations were evident. Classification results can be highly sensitive to the quality and representativeness of training data, and different methods may produce varying outputs for the same dataset. In addition, there is an inherent level of subjectivity in defining classes and training samples, which can introduce bias into the results. These limitations highlight the importance of critically evaluating classification outputs rather than assuming they are inherently accurate.








Applications



Classification techniques are widely used to translate remote sensing imagery into structured and interpretable outputs that can support analysis and decision-making. By assigning pixels to defined categories, such as land cover types, classification enables large volumes of imagery to be summarised in a way that is more accessible and usable within wider analytical workflows.
One of the most common applications of classification is in land cover mapping, where satellite imagery is used to identify and monitor changes in urban areas, vegetation, and water bodies. Supervised classification methods, in particular, are frequently used where training data is available, allowing models to be tailored to specific contexts and objectives. This approach has been widely applied in environmental monitoring and urban studies, where classification outputs provide a consistent way to assess spatial patterns and change over time (e.g. Foody (2002)).
Classification is also increasingly used in automated and large-scale analysis, particularly with the integration of machine learning approaches. These methods allow for more complex patterns within the data to be identified, improving classification accuracy when compared to more traditional techniques. For example, machine learning-based classification has been shown to improve the extraction of land cover information from remotely sensed data, particularly when dealing with heterogeneous or complex environments (e.g. Belgiu & Drăguţ (2016)).
However, the effectiveness of classification in these applications depends heavily on the quality of input data and the selection of training samples. In urban environments like London in particular, classification can be more challenging due to the relative homogeneity of materials, where surfaces such as roads, rooftops, and paved areas often exhibit similar spectral characteristics. This can make it difficult to distinguish between classes that are visually and spectrally alike, increasing the likelihood of misclassification. As a result, higher resolution data, additional spectral bands, or more advanced classification approaches are often required to improve accuracy in city-based contexts. Poorly defined training data or inappropriate classification methods can therefore lead to inaccurate or misleading outputs, which can affect downstream analysis and decision-making. This highlights the importance of combining technical methods with contextual understanding to ensure that classification results are both accurate and meaningful in real-world applications.








Reflection



This week highlighted that while classification can appear straightforward in theory, applying it in practice is far more nuanced. The process of defining training data and selecting appropriate classes made it clear that results are heavily influenced by the decisions made at the outset. Small changes in training samples or class definitions can lead to noticeably different outputs, which reinforced that classification is not purely objective, but requires judgement and iteration.
What stood out most was how challenging classification becomes in urban environments. Unlike more distinct natural landscapes, cities contain a mix of materials that often appear very similar in remotely sensed data. This makes it difficult to clearly separate classes such as different types of built surfaces, and highlights the limitations of relying solely on spectral information. It also emphasised the importance of understanding the local context when interpreting results, rather than taking outputs at face value.
From a professional perspective, this has clear relevance to my role working in a London borough. There is potential to use classification techniques to support areas such as monitoring land use, identifying changes in urban form, or supporting policy objectives linked to the London Plan. It could also support collaboration with colleagues such as ecologists and climate teams, for example in identifying green space, monitoring vegetation change, or providing supporting evidence within work such as Strategic Flood Risk Assessments (SFRA). However, this would require careful consideration of how classes are defined to align with planning and environmental categories, as well as validation to ensure outputs are reliable enough to inform decision-making.
Looking ahead, as my understanding of remote sensing develops, there is an opportunity to explore how classification could be integrated into existing workflows to provide additional spatial insight. Given that this area is not yet widely used within my organisation, there is also potential to shape how it is introduced and applied. This makes it important to develop not only the technical skills required, but also an understanding of how to translate classification outputs into information that is meaningful and usable within a planning and policy context.
crumbs: 6  Week 7 – Classification

title: 7  Classification: The Big Questions and Accuracy
text:
7.1 Weekly Learning Diary
:::
crumbs: 7  Week 8 – Classification: The Big Questions and Accuracy

title: 7  Classification: The Big Questions and Accuracy
text:
Summary



Building directly on Week 7, this week focused on evaluating the outputs of classification rather than generating them. While the previous week introduced methods for assigning pixels to classes, this week explored how to assess the accuracy and reliability of those classifications, highlighting that producing a map is only one part of the process, and that understanding its limitations is equally important.
A key concept introduced was the use of confusion matrices to compare classified outputs against reference data, allowing for the calculation of metrics such as overall accuracy, producer’s accuracy, and user’s accuracy. These measures provide insight into different types of error, such as omission and commission, and help to identify where classifications may be performing poorly. This reinforced that accuracy is not a single value, but something that needs to be interpreted carefully depending on the intended application.
The practical component involved applying these concepts to assess classification outputs, including generating accuracy metrics and interpreting the results. This highlighted how even classifications that appear visually correct can contain significant errors when tested against reference data, emphasising the importance of validation as a critical step in the workflow.
However, several limitations were evident. Accuracy assessment relies heavily on the quality and representativeness of reference data, which can be difficult to obtain, particularly in complex urban environments. Additionally, there is often a trade-off between classification detail and accuracy, where increasing the number of classes can lead to greater uncertainty. These challenges demonstrate that classification outputs should not be treated as definitive, but rather as informed interpretations that require careful validation and contextual understanding.








Applications



The evaluation of classification accuracy is critical in ensuring that remote sensing outputs can be used reliably in real-world decision-making. In many applications, classification results are used to inform policy, planning, and environmental management, meaning that errors within the data can have direct consequences. Accuracy assessment therefore plays a key role in determining whether outputs are suitable for their intended use, rather than assuming that all classifications are equally reliable.
One important application of accuracy assessment is in environmental monitoring, where classification outputs are used to track changes in land cover over time. In these contexts, inaccuracies can lead to misinterpretation of trends, such as overestimating vegetation loss or failing to detect important environmental changes. Studies have shown that classification uncertainty can significantly influence the conclusions drawn from remotely sensed data, particularly when used for long-term monitoring and policy support (e.g. Olofsson et al. (2014)).
Accuracy assessment is also essential when classification outputs are used within operational or planning contexts. For example, when classifications are integrated into GIS systems or used to support spatial analysis, errors can propagate through workflows and affect subsequent analysis or modelling. This is particularly important in urban environments, where small misclassifications can have disproportionate impacts due to the density and complexity of land use. As a result, understanding and communicating uncertainty becomes as important as the classification itself, ensuring that decision-makers are aware of the limitations of the data they are using.
Ultimately, accuracy assessment enables classification outputs to move from being purely analytical products to tools that can be trusted and applied in practice. By quantifying uncertainty and identifying sources of error, it supports more informed and responsible use of remote sensing data across a range of applications.








Reflection



This week shifted my perspective from producing classification outputs to questioning how much they can actually be trusted. While it is relatively straightforward to generate a classification map, assessing its accuracy introduced a level of uncertainty that is easy to overlook. The use of confusion matrices and accuracy metrics made it clear that even outputs that appear visually correct can contain significant errors, reinforcing the importance of validation before any real-world use.
What I found particularly interesting was how different types of accuracy reveal different issues within the data. For example, understanding the difference between omission and commission errors highlighted that some classes may consistently be over- or under-represented, depending on how the model has been trained. This made me realise that accuracy is not just about achieving a high overall percentage, but about understanding where and why errors occur, and whether those errors matter for the intended application.
From a professional perspective, this has clear implications for how remotely sensed data could be used within a local authority setting such as Brent. If classification outputs were to be used to support planning, environmental monitoring, or policy development, it would be essential to understand their limitations before integrating them into decision-making processes. There is a risk that outputs could be taken at face value, particularly by non-technical stakeholders, which could lead to misinformed decisions if accuracy is not properly communicated.
This also highlighted the importance of how results are presented and explained. It is not enough to produce technically accurate outputs; there is a responsibility to clearly communicate uncertainty and limitations in a way that is accessible to others. In a council environment, where outputs may be used across different teams, this becomes even more important to ensure that data is used appropriately and responsibly.
Overall, this week reinforced that accuracy assessment is not just a technical step, but a critical part of ensuring that remote sensing outputs are fit for purpose. As I continue developing my skills in this area, I will need to focus not only on producing outputs, but on understanding their reliability and communicating that effectively within a real-world context.
crumbs: 7  Week 8 – Classification: The Big Questions and Accuracy

title: 8  SAR in Google Earth Engine
text:
8.1 Weekly Learning Diary
:::
crumbs: 8  Week 9 – SAR in Google Earth Engine

title: 8  SAR in Google Earth Engine
text:
Summary



This week introduced the use of remote sensing for measuring surface temperature, focusing on how thermal data can be used to analyse environmental and urban conditions. Unlike previous weeks which focused on classification and accuracy, this topic explored how remotely sensed data can be used to derive physical properties of the Earth’s surface, specifically land surface temperature (LST).
A key concept was the use of thermal infrared sensors, such as those available on Landsat, which detect emitted radiation rather than reflected energy. This distinguishes thermal remote sensing from optical approaches, as it enables the measurement of surface temperature based on the intensity of emitted radiation. The process of deriving land surface temperature involved several steps, including converting digital numbers to radiance, then to brightness temperature, and applying corrections to account for emissivity.
The practical component focused on applying these steps to Landsat imagery to calculate land surface temperature and explore spatial patterns. This provided insight into how temperature varies across different land cover types, particularly within urban environments where materials such as concrete and asphalt retain heat differently to vegetation. This highlighted the concept of the urban heat island effect, where built-up areas tend to experience higher temperatures than surrounding rural areas.
However, there are important limitations to consider. Thermal data is influenced by factors such as atmospheric conditions, surface emissivity, and the time of image capture, all of which can affect accuracy. Additionally, land surface temperature does not directly represent air temperature, which is often more relevant for human experience. These limitations highlight the need to carefully interpret thermal outputs and understand what is actually being measured when applying these techniques in real-world contexts.








Applications



Thermal remote sensing plays an important role in understanding and managing urban environments, particularly in relation to heat and climate. By deriving land surface temperature from satellite imagery, it is possible to identify spatial patterns of heat across cities and highlight areas that may be more vulnerable to extreme temperatures. This has become increasingly important in the context of climate change, where urban heat is recognised as a growing risk to public health and infrastructure.
One key application is the analysis of the urban heat island effect, where built-up areas experience higher temperatures than surrounding rural regions due to the concentration of heat-retaining materials such as concrete and asphalt. Remote sensing provides a consistent and scalable way to map these patterns, supporting interventions such as increasing urban greening, improving surface materials, or targeting areas most at risk. Studies have shown that satellite-derived land surface temperature can be effectively used to assess urban heat patterns and inform mitigation strategies (e.g. Voogt & Oke (2003)).
These approaches also have practical applications within planning and policy contexts. For example, temperature data can be integrated into spatial analysis to support decisions around urban design, green infrastructure, and climate resilience. When combined with other datasets, such as land use or vegetation cover, thermal data can help to identify priority areas for intervention and support evidence-based policy development. This highlights the value of remote sensing not just as an analytical tool, but as a way to inform real-world decision-making in response to environmental challenges.
However, the application of thermal remote sensing must be approached carefully. Land surface temperature represents the temperature of surfaces rather than the air, meaning it may not directly reflect human thermal experience. Additionally, factors such as time of data capture and surface characteristics can influence results. This reinforces the importance of understanding the limitations of the data when applying it within policy and planning contexts.








Reflection



This week shifted the focus from classifying land cover to understanding physical properties of the Earth’s surface, which felt like a significant step forward in terms of real-world application. Rather than assigning categories, deriving land surface temperature provided a way to quantify environmental conditions, making the outputs feel more directly relevant to current challenges such as climate change and urban heat.
What stood out most was how clearly temperature patterns reflect the structure of the urban environment. Seeing higher temperatures associated with built-up areas and lower temperatures linked to vegetation reinforced the importance of green infrastructure in managing urban heat. It also highlighted how remote sensing can provide evidence to support interventions, rather than relying solely on assumptions about how cities behave.
From a professional perspective, this has clear relevance to work within a London borough, particularly in relation to climate resilience and planning. There is potential to use thermal data to better understand where heat is concentrated and to support initiatives aimed at reducing urban heat impacts, such as increasing tree cover or improving public spaces. This links closely to wider climate and sustainability work, where having spatial evidence can strengthen decision-making and help target interventions more effectively.
However, this week also reinforced the importance of understanding what the data is actually showing. The distinction between land surface temperature and air temperature is particularly important, as it would be easy to misinterpret the results without this awareness. This emphasised that while remote sensing provides powerful insights, it must be used carefully and with a clear understanding of its limitations.
Overall, this week highlighted how remote sensing can move beyond mapping and classification to provide meaningful environmental insights. As my understanding develops, I can see clear opportunities to apply these techniques within a local government context, particularly in supporting climate-related work, while also recognising the importance of interpreting the outputs responsibly.
crumbs: 8  Week 9 – SAR in Google Earth Engine

title: 2  Xaringan and Quarto
text:
Summary



This week focused on the tools and workflows required to present and communicate work effectively, introducing Quarto and Xaringan as key platforms for building reproducible and shareable outputs. Rather than focusing on remote sensing concepts directly, the emphasis was on how analysis can be structured, documented, and presented in a clear and accessible way.
A key theme was the importance of reproducibility, ensuring that outputs can be consistently regenerated and updated as data or methods change. Quarto provides a framework for integrating code, text, and outputs into a single document, while Xaringan enables the creation of interactive presentations using R Markdown. Together, these tools support a more transparent and efficient workflow, particularly when working with data-driven analysis.
The practical component involved setting up a portfolio structure and creating a Xaringan presentation, which will be embedded within the portfolio. This required understanding how different components link together, including file structure, rendering, and hosting outputs via GitHub Pages.
However, there are limitations to consider. Setting up these tools requires an initial investment in time to understand syntax, structure, and dependencies, which can be challenging for beginners. Additionally, ensuring that outputs render correctly across environments can be difficult, particularly when integrating multiple tools. Despite this, the long-term benefits of reproducibility and structured workflows make these tools valuable for managing and presenting analytical work.








Applications



The use of tools such as Quarto and Xaringan has important applications in managing and communicating analytical work, particularly in environments where outputs need to be shared across different audiences. By combining code, analysis, and narrative in a single workflow, these tools support more transparent and reproducible approaches to working with data.
One key application is in reporting and documentation, where reproducible documents can be used to ensure that outputs remain consistent as data is updated. This is particularly useful in environments where analysis is repeated regularly, as it reduces the need for manual updates and minimises the risk of errors. It also allows for clearer auditability, as the steps used to generate outputs are explicitly documented.
These tools are also valuable for presenting complex information in a more accessible format. Interactive presentations and structured reports make it easier to communicate findings to non-technical audiences, bridging the gap between analysis and decision-making. This is particularly relevant in professional contexts where outputs need to be clearly understood by a range of stakeholders.
Overall, the ability to create reproducible and well-structured outputs supports more efficient workflows and improves the communication of analytical results, making these tools an important component of modern data-driven work.








Reflection



This week highlighted the importance of not just carrying out analysis, but being able to present it clearly and effectively. While the technical side of remote sensing is important, the ability to communicate results in a structured and reproducible way is equally valuable, particularly when working in professional environments.
I found the introduction to Quarto relatively intuitive, especially in terms of structuring content and building a portfolio. However, working with Xaringan was more challenging, particularly in understanding how presentations are generated and embedded. This required a different way of thinking compared to more traditional tools like PowerPoint, but it also demonstrated the potential for creating more dynamic and integrated outputs.
From a professional perspective, these tools have clear relevance. In a local authority setting such as Brent, there is often a need to present analysis in a way that is both clear and reproducible, particularly when outputs are shared across teams or used to support decision-making. Being able to integrate analysis, documentation, and presentation into a single workflow could improve both efficiency and transparency.
Overall, this week reinforced that strong analytical work is not just about producing results, but about how those results are communicated and maintained over time. Developing these skills will be important in ensuring that technical outputs can be effectively used in real-world contexts.
crumbs: 2  Week 2 – Portfolio Tools: Xaringan and Quarto
